does: This paper introduces harm recovery, a framework for steering language model agents back to safe states after they execute harmful actions on computer systems. The authors conduct a formative user study with 1,130 pairwise judgments to understand human preferences for recovery strategies, identifying context-dependent dimensions like pragmatism and targeting specificity over comprehensive remediation. They develop a reward model trained on these preferences to re-rank candidate recovery plans generated at test time, and introduce BackBench, a benchmark of 50 computer-use tasks designed to evaluate agent recovery capabilities from harmful states. Human evaluation demonstrates the effectiveness of preference-aligned recovery compared to baseline approaches.
connects: This work extends the line of research on AI safety and alignment for autonomous agents, building on prior work in post-deployment safeguards and preference learning from human feedback. It addresses a gap in the literature focused on harm prevention, shifting attention to the underexplored problem of remediation when preventive measures fail. The approach combines techniques from learning from human preferences and reward modeling with interactive evaluation methodologies developed in AI safety. It also connects to broader work on computer-use agents and their grounding in real-world execution environments, where the stakes of unintended consequences are particularly high and require systematic safety protocols.
enables: This work establishes foundations for scaling harm recovery across diverse computer-use scenarios, opening pathways to more robust safeguards for autonomous agent deployment. Future work can build on the identified recovery dimensions and preference patterns to develop more sophisticated recovery strategies that adapt to task context and severity levels. The BackBench benchmark enables systematic evaluation of recovery capabilities and could drive development of agents with built-in recovery planning. Additionally, this framework could extend to other domains beyond computer use, informing how language models might autonomously remediate harms in real-world applications, and eventually contribute to more trustworthy deployment of increasingly capable AI systems in high-stakes environments.
